content: If one of the diameters of the circle, given by the equation $x^2+y^2-4 x+6 y-12=0$, is a chord of a circle, ' S ', whose centre is at $(-3,2)$, then the length of radius of ' S ' is _______ units.
explanation:
The centre of the given circle is $\mathrm{C}_1(2,-3)$ and radius

$$ \begin{aligned} & \quad=\sqrt{(-2)^2+3^2-(-12)} \\ & =5 \\ & \mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{C}_2=\sqrt{(-3-2)^2+(2+3)^2} \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =\sqrt{25+25} \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =\sqrt{50} \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Radius of } \mathrm{S} \text { is } \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{~A}=\sqrt{(\sqrt{50})^2+5^2} \\ &\\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =\sqrt{75} \\ & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=5 \sqrt{3} \end{aligned} $$

content: The least distance of the point $\mathrm{A}(10,7)$ from the circle $x^2+y^2-4 x-2 y-20=0$ is length of seg AM . If $\mathrm{MM}^{\prime}$ is the diameter of the circle, then the lengths of AM and $\mathrm{AM}^{\prime}$ are respectively ___________ , ____________units
explanation:
Given equation of circle is

$$ x^2+y^2-4 x-2 y-20=0 $$

Here, $\mathrm{g}=-2, \mathrm{f}=-1, \mathrm{c}=-20$

Radius $(\mathrm{r})=\sqrt{\mathrm{g}^2+\mathrm{f}^2-\mathrm{c}}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\sqrt{4+1+20} \\ & =5 \text { units } \end{aligned} $$

Centre $(\mathrm{C})=(2,1)$

$$ \begin{aligned} d(A C)=\sqrt{(10-2)^2+(7-1)^2} & =\sqrt{8^2+6^2}=\sqrt{100} \\ & =10 \text { units } \end{aligned} $$

Shortest distance of point A from circle

= Distance of point A from centre - radius

$=10-5$

$=5$ units

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow A M=5 \text { units } \\ & A M^{\prime}=5+\text { diameter }=15 \text { units } \end{aligned} $$

content: If a circle with centre at $(-1,1)$ touches the line $x+2 y+4=0$ then the co-ordinates of the point of contact are
explanation:
Let the point of contact be $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})$.

Then, $h+2 k+4=0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$

Slope of $x+2 y+4=0$ is $\frac{-1}{2}$.

OP is perpendicular to $x+2 y+4=0$.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \left(\frac{k-1}{h+1}\right)\left(\frac{-1}{2}\right)=-1 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 h-k=-3 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{array} $$

Solving (i) and (ii), we get $\mathrm{h}=-2$ and $\mathrm{k}=-1$

content: A pair of tangents are drawn to the circle $x^2+y^2+6 x-4 y-12=0$ from a point $\mathrm{P}(-4,-5)$, then the area enclosed between these tangents and the area of the circle is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & x^2+y^2+6 x-4 y-12=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x+3)^2+(y-2)^2=25 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Centre $=(-3,2)$ and radius $=5$

Length of tangent segment from $\mathrm{P}(-4,-5)$ is

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\sqrt{(-4)^2+(-5)^2+6(-4)-4(-5)-12} \\ & =\sqrt{25} \\ & =5 \end{aligned} $$

Area of quadrilateral PACB

$=2 \times$ Area of $\triangle \mathrm{PAC}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =2 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 5 \\ & =25 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Area of sector } \mathrm{CAQB}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{r}^2 \theta \\ & \quad\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=\frac{1}{2}(5)^2 \frac{\pi}{2}=\frac{25 \pi}{4} \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Required area }=25-\frac{25 \pi}{4} \\ & \quad\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=25\left(\frac{4-\pi}{4}\right) \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} $$

content: The equations of the tangents to the circle $x^2+y^2=36$ which are perpendicular to the line $5 x+y-2=0$ are
explanation:
Given equation of circle is

$$ x^2+y^2=36 \Rightarrow a=6 $$

Slope of $5 x+y=2$ is -5

$\therefore \quad$ Slope of required line $=\frac{1}{5}$

Equations of tangents to the circle $x^2+y^2=\mathrm{a}^2$ is

$$ \begin{aligned} & y=\mathrm{m} x \pm \sqrt{\mathrm{a}^2\left(1+\mathrm{m}^2\right)} \\ & \Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{5} x \pm \sqrt{36\left(1+\frac{1}{25}\right)} \\ & \Rightarrow 5 y=x \pm 6 \sqrt{26} \\ & \Rightarrow x-5 y \pm 6 \sqrt{26}=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: The equations of the tangents to the circle $x^2+y^2=36$ which are perpendicular to the line $5 x+y=2$, are
explanation:

content: If the tangent and the normal at the point $(\sqrt{3}, 1)$ to the circle $x^2+y^{2 }=4$, and the X -axis form a triangle, then the area (in sq.units) of this triangle is
explanation:
Given equation of circle $x^2+y^2=4$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 x+2 y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{-x}{y} \\ & \left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{\operatorname{att}(\sqrt{3}, 1)}=\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{1}=-\sqrt{3} \end{aligned} $$

$\Rightarrow$ Slope of tangent $\left(\mathrm{m}_1\right)=-\sqrt{3}$

and slope of normal $\left(m_2\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$

$$ \therefore \text { Equation of tangent at }(\sqrt{3}, 1) \text {, is given by } $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & (y-1)=-\sqrt{3}(x-\sqrt{3}) \\ & y-1=-\sqrt{3} x+3 \\ & y+\sqrt{3} x=4 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Intersection of tangent on X-axis $\Rightarrow y=0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow x=\frac{4}{\sqrt{3}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots [From (i)] \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~B}(x, y)=\left(\frac{4}{\sqrt{3}}, 0\right) \end{aligned} $$

Equation of normal at $(\sqrt{3}, 1)$ is

$$ \begin{aligned} & y-1=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(x-\sqrt{3}) \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{3} y-\sqrt{3}=x-\sqrt{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{3} y=x \end{aligned} $$

Intersection of normal on X -axis is, $(x, y)=(0,0)$.

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Area of triangle } & =\frac{1}{2} \times \text { base } \times \text { height } \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{4}{\sqrt{3}} \times 1=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \text { sq. units. } \end{aligned} $$

content: The locus of point of intersection of the tangents to the circle $x^2+y^2=16$, such that the angle between them is $60^{\circ}$, is
explanation:
$$ \text { Given equation of circle is } $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & x^2+y^2=16 \\ & C \equiv(0,0) \\ & r=4 \text { units } \\ & \text { In } \triangle A P O, \\ & \angle P=30^{\circ} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \sin 30^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{OA}}{\mathrm{OP}} \\ & \frac{1}{2}=\frac{4}{\mathrm{OP}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{OP}=8 \text { units } \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{(x-0)+(y-0)^2}=8 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2+y^2=8^2 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2+y^2=64 \end{aligned} $$

content: The minimum distance and maximum distance of the point $\mathrm{P}(2,-7)$ from the circle $x^2+y^2-14 x-10 y-151=0$ are respectively _______units
explanation:
Given,

$$ \begin{aligned} & x^2+y^2-14 x-10 y-151=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(x^2-14 x\right)+\left(y^2-10 y\right)=151 \\ & \Rightarrow\left((x-7)^2-49\right)+\left((y-5)^2-25\right)=151 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-7)^2+(y-5)^2=151+74 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-7)^2+(y-5)^2=225 \end{aligned} $$

Comparing with $(x-\mathrm{h})^2+(y-\mathrm{k})^2=\mathrm{r}^2$, we get Centre $=(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})=(7,5)$ and radius $(\mathrm{r})=15$

Now,

Distance of point P from the centre of circle

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\sqrt{(7-2)^2+(5-(-7))^2} \\ & =\sqrt{25+144} \\ & =\sqrt{169} \\ & =13 \end{aligned} $$

Let $\mathrm{d}=$ distance from point P to centre $=13$

$$ \therefore $$ Minimum distance $=|\mathrm{r}-\mathrm{d}|=|15-13|=2$

Maximum distance $=|\mathrm{r}+\mathrm{d}|=|15+13|=28$

content: The number of integral values of $k$ for which $x^2+y^2+\mathrm{k} x+(1-\mathrm{k}) y+5=0$ represents a circle whose radius cannot exceeds 5 , are
explanation:
Given, $x^2+y^2+\mathrm{k} x+(1-\mathrm{k}) y+5=0$

Comparing with

$x^2+y^2+2 g x+2 f y+c=0$, we get

$$ g=\frac{k}{2}, f=\frac{1-k}{2}, c=5 $$

According to the given condition, Radius $\leq 5$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \sqrt{\left(\frac{k}{2}\right)^2+\left(\frac{1-k}{2}\right)^2-5} \leq 5 \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{\frac{2 k^2-2 k-19}{4}} \leq 5 \end{aligned} $$

Squaring on both sides, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{2 k^2-2 k-19}{4} \leq 25 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 k^2-2 k-119 \leq 0 \end{aligned} $$

Consider,

$$ 2 k^2-2 k-119=0 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow k=\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{239}}{2}=\frac{1 \pm 15.46}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow k=8.23 \text { or } k=-7.23 \\ & \Rightarrow-7.23 \leq k \leq 8.23 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ The integral values of k are

$$ -7,-6,-5, \ldots ., 6,7,8 $$

$\therefore \quad$ The number of integral values of $\mathrm{k}=16$

content: The number of common tangents that can be drawn to the circles $x^2+y^2-6 x=0$ and $x^2+y^2+6 x+2 y+1=0$ is __________
explanation:
Given circles:

First circle: $x^2 + y^2 - 6x = 0$

Second circle: $x^2 + y^2 + 6x + 2y + 1 = 0$

Step 1: Write in standard form

First circle:

$ x^2 + y^2 - 6x = 0 \implies x^2 - 6x + y^2 = 0 $

Complete the square for $x$:

$ x^2 - 6x = (x - 3)^2 - 9 $

So,

$ (x - 3)^2 - 9 + y^2 = 0 \implies (x - 3)^2 + y^2 = 9 $

Centre: $(3, 0)$, Radius: $3$

Second circle:

$ x^2 + y^2 + 6x + 2y + 1 = 0 $

$ x^2 + 6x + y^2 + 2y = -1 $

Complete the square:

  • For $x$: $x^2 + 6x = (x+3)^2 - 9$

  • For $y$: $y^2 + 2y = (y+1)^2 - 1$

So,

$ (x+3)^2 - 9 + (y+1)^2 - 1 = -1 $

$ (x+3)^2 + (y+1)^2 - 10 = -1 $

$ (x+3)^2 + (y+1)^2 = 9 $

Centre: $(-3, -1)$, Radius: $3$

Step 2: Find the Distance between Centres

Centres are $(3, 0)$ and $(-3, -1)$.

$ \text{Distance} = \sqrt{ (3 - (-3))^2 + (0 - (-1))^2 } = \sqrt{ (6)^2 + (1)^2 } = \sqrt{36 + 1} = \sqrt{37} $

Step 3: Check Position of Circles

Let $r_1 = 3$, $r_2 = 3$, and $d = \sqrt{37}$.

  • If $d > r_1 + r_2$, $4$ tangents.

  • If $d = r_1 + r_2$, $3$ tangents.

  • If $|r_1 - r_2| < d < r_1 + r_2$, $2$ tangents.

  • If $d = |r_1 - r_2|$, $1$ tangent.

  • If $d < |r_1 - r_2|$, $0$ tangents.

Here,

$ r_1 + r_2 = 3 + 3 = 6 $

$ d = \sqrt{37} \approx 6.08 $

So, $d > r_1 + r_2$.

Step 4: Conclusion

Number of common tangents = $4$.

Correct answer: Option D — 4

content: One end of the diameter of the circle $x^2+y^2-6 x-5 y-1=0$ is $(-1,3)$, then the equation of the tangent at the other end of the diameter is
explanation:
If $\left(x_1, y_1\right)$ is one end of a diameter of the circle $x^2+y^2+2 \mathrm{~g} x+2 \mathrm{f} y+\mathrm{c}=0$, then the other end is $\left[-\left(x_1+2 \mathrm{~g}\right),-\left(y_1+2 \mathrm{f}\right)\right]$

$\therefore \quad$ The other end of $x^2+y^2-6 x-5 y-1=0$ is $[-(-1-6),-(3-5)]$ i.e. $(7,2)$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of tangent at $(7,2)$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & 7 x+2 y-3(x+7)-\frac{5}{2}(y+2)-1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 8 x-y-54=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation of the circle, concentric with the circle $2 x^2+2 y^2-6 x+8 y+1=0$ and double of its area is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & 2 x^2+2 y^2-6 x+8 y+1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2+y^2-3 x+4 y+\frac{1}{2}=0 \end{aligned}$$

Equation of circle concentric to given circle is $$x^2+y^2-3 x+4 y+k=0$$

Since area of required circle $=2($ area of given circle $)$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \pi r_2^2=2\left(\pi r_1^2\right) \\ & \Rightarrow r_2=\sqrt{2} \mathrm{r}_1 \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{\left(-\frac{3}{2}\right)^2+2^2-\mathrm{k}}=\sqrt{2} \sqrt{\left(\frac{-3}{2}\right)^2+2^2-\frac{1}{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{\frac{25}{4}-\mathrm{k}}=\sqrt{2} \sqrt{\frac{23}{4}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{25}{4}-\mathrm{k}=\frac{23}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{-21}{4} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Hence, the required equation is }\\ &\begin{aligned} & x^2+y^2-3 x+4 y-\frac{21}{4}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 x^2+4 y^2-12 x+16 y-21=0 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the sides of a rectangle are given by the equations $x=-2, x=6, y=-2, y=5$, then the equation of the circle, drawn on the diagonal of this rectangle as its diameter, is
explanation:
The given equations of the sides are $x=-2, x=6, y=-2$ and $y=5$.

Here, the diagonals AC and BD of rectangle $A B C D$ are diameters of the circle passing through the vertices $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{D}$.

Considering diagonal AC with end points $\mathrm{A}(-2,-2)$ and $\mathrm{C}(6,5)$, we get

Equation of circle in diameter form as,

$$\begin{aligned} & (x-6)(x+2)+(y-5)(y+2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2-4 x-12+y^2-3 y-10=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2+y^2-4 x-3 y-22=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The number of common tangents to the circles $x^2+y^2-x=0$ and $x^2+y^2+x=0$ is /are
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & x^2+y^2-x=0 \\ & \mathrm{C}_1=\left(\frac{1}{2}, 0\right), \mathrm{r}_1=\sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+0^2-0}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & x^2+y^2+x=0 \\ & \mathrm{C}_2=\left(-\frac{1}{2}, 0\right), \mathrm{r}_2=\sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+0^2-0}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{C}_2=\sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+0^2}=1 \\ & \mathrm{r}_1+\mathrm{r}_2=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}=1 \\ \therefore\quad& \mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{C}_2=\mathrm{r}_1 \mathrm{r}_2 \\ & \Rightarrow \text { The given circles touch each other externally. } \\ & \Rightarrow \text { Number of common tangents }=3 \end{aligned}$$

content: The parametric equations of the circle $x^2+y^2-\mathrm{a} x-b y=0$ are
explanation:
Given equation can be written as

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad\left(x^2-\mathrm{ax}+\frac{\mathrm{a}^2}{4}\right)+\left(y^2-\mathrm{b} y+\frac{\mathrm{b}^2}{4}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{a}^2}{4}+\frac{\mathrm{b}^2}{4} \\ & \quad \Rightarrow\left(x-\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}\right)^2+\left(y-\frac{\mathrm{b}}{2}\right)^2=\left(\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2}{4}}\right)^2 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{~h}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}, \mathrm{k}=\frac{\mathrm{b}}{2} \text { and } \mathrm{r}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2}{4}} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Parametric equations of circle are

$x=\frac{a}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}{2} \cos \theta$ and

$$y=\frac{\mathrm{b}}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2}}{2} \sin \theta$$

content: The equation of the tangent to the circle, given by $x=5 \cos \theta, y=5 \sin \theta$ at the point $\theta=\frac{\pi}{3}$ on it , is
explanation:
The equation of the tangent to the circle $x^2+y^2=\mathrm{a}^2$ at $\mathrm{P}(\theta)$ is $x \cos \theta+y \sin \theta=\mathrm{a}$

Here, $a=5, \theta=\frac{\pi}{3}$

$\therefore \quad$ The equation of the tangent is

$$\begin{aligned} & x \cos \frac{\pi}{3}+y \sin \frac{\pi}{3}=5 \\ & \Rightarrow x\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)+y\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)=5 \\ & \Rightarrow x+y \sqrt{3}=10 \end{aligned}$$

content: Let PQ and RS be tangents at the extremities of the diameter PR of a circle of radius $r$. If PS and RQ intersect at a point X on the circumference of the circle, then 2 r equals

explanation:
Consider the given figure,

In $\triangle \mathrm{PXR}, \angle \mathrm{X}=90^{\circ}$

$\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{PRX}=90^{\circ}-\theta\quad\text{.... (i)}$

$\therefore \quad$ In $\triangle \mathrm{PRS}$,

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \tan \theta=\frac{R S}{P R}=\frac{R S}{2 r} \quad\text{.... (ii)}\\ & \ln \triangle P R Q, \angle P R Q=90^{\circ}-\theta \quad\text{... [from (i)]}\\ \therefore & \tan \left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)=\frac{P Q}{P R}=\frac{P Q}{2 r} \\ \therefore & \cot \theta=\frac{P Q}{2 r} \\ \therefore & \tan \theta=\frac{2 r}{P Q}\quad\text{.... content: The abscissae of the two points A and B are the roots of the equation $x^2+2 a x-b^2=0$ and their ordinates are roots of the equation $y^2+2 p y-q^2=0$. Then the equation of the circle with AB as diameter is given by
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{A} \equiv\left(x_1, y_1\right)$ and $\mathrm{B} \equiv\left(x_2, y_2\right)$.

According to the given condition,

$$\begin{aligned} & x_1+x_2=-2 \mathrm{a}, x_1 x_2=-\mathrm{b}^2 \\ & y_1+y_2=-2 \mathrm{p}, y_1 y_2=-\mathrm{q}^2 \end{aligned}$$

The equation of the circle with $\mathrm{A}\left(x_1, y_1\right)$ and $\mathrm{B}\left(x_2, y_2\right)$ as the end points of diameter is

$$\begin{aligned} & \left(x-x_1\right)\left(x-x_2\right)+\left(y-y_1\right)\left(y-y_2\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2+y^2-x\left(x_1+x_2\right)-y\left(y_1+y_2\right)+x_1 x_2+y_1 y_2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2+y^2+2 \mathrm{a} x+2 \mathrm{p} y-\mathrm{b}^2-\mathrm{q}^2=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation of the concentric circle, with the circle $\mathrm{C}_1$ having equation $x^2+y^2-6 x-4 y-12=0$ and having double area compared to the area of $\mathrm{C}_1$, is
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} & x^2+y^2-6 x-4 y-12=0 \\ \therefore \quad & \left(x^2-6 x+9-9\right)+\left(y^2-4 y+4-4\right)-12=0 \\ \therefore \quad & (x-3)^2+(y-2)^2=25 \end{array}$$

$\therefore \quad$ for circle $\mathrm{C}_1:$ Centre is $(3,2)$ and radius $=5$

$\therefore \quad$ Area of $\mathrm{C}_1=\pi \mathrm{r}^2=25 \pi$

Let the radius of required circle be R .

Area of required circle $=2\left(\right.$ Area of $\left.C_1\right)$

$\therefore \quad \pi \mathrm{R}^2=2(25 \pi)$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{R}^2=50$

content: Two tangents drawn from $\mathrm{P}(1,7)$ to the circle $x^2+y^2=25$, touch the circle at Q and R respectively. The area of the quadrilateral PQOR is

content: If $\left(m_i, \frac{1}{m_i}\right), m_i>0, i=1,2,3,4$ are four distinct points on a circle, then the product $\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2 \mathrm{~m}_3 \mathrm{~m}_4$ is equal to
explanation:
Let $\left(m_i, \frac{1}{m_i}\right)$ lie on the circle $x^2+y^2+2 \mathrm{~g} x+2 \mathrm{f} y+\mathrm{c}=0$, where $\mathrm{i}=1,2,3,4$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad\left(m_i\right)^2+\left(\frac{1}{m_i}\right)^2+2 \mathrm{gm}_{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{2 \mathrm{f}}{\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{i}}}+\mathrm{c}=0 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{i}}^2+\frac{1}{\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{i}}^2}+2 \mathrm{gm}_{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{2 \mathrm{f}}{\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{i}}}+\mathrm{c}=0 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{i}}^4+1+2 \mathrm{gm}_{\mathrm{i}}^3+2 \mathrm{fm}_{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{cm}_{\mathrm{i}}^2+2=0 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{i}}^4+2 \mathrm{gm}_{\mathrm{i}}^3+\mathrm{cm}_{\mathrm{i}}^2+2 \mathrm{fm}_{\mathrm{i}}+1=0 \end{aligned}$$

$\mathrm{m}_1, \mathrm{~m}_2, \mathrm{~m}_3, \mathrm{~m}_4$ are roots of the above equation

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \text { product of roots }=\frac{e}{a}=\frac{1}{1}=1 \\ & \therefore \quad m_1 m_2 m_3 m_4=1 \end{aligned}$$

content: If the line $$x-2 y=\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{m} \in \mathrm{Z})$$ intersects the circle $$x^2+y^2=2 x+4 y$$ at two distinct points, then the number of possible values of $m$ are

radius $$=\sqrt{1+4-0}=\sqrt{5}$$

content: The abscissae of two points $$A$$ and $$B$$ are the roots of the equation $$x^2+2 a x-b^2=0$$ and their ordinates are roots of the equation $$y^2+2 p y-q^2=0$$. Then, the equation of the circle with $$A B$$ as diameter is given by
explanation:
Here, $$x^2+2 a x-b^2=0$$

Let roots be $$x_1$$ and $$x_2$$

$$\begin{aligned} & x_1+x_2=\frac{-b}{a}=-2 a \\ & x_1 \cdot x_2=\frac{c}{a}=-b^2 \end{aligned}$$

and $$y^2+2 p y-q^2=0$$

Let roots be $$y_1$$ and $$y_2$$

$$\begin{aligned} y_1+y_2 & =-2 p \\ y_1 \cdot y_2 & =-q^2 \end{aligned}$$

Equation in diametric form is

$$\begin{aligned} & x^2+y^2-\left(x_1+x_2\right) x-\left(y_1+y_2\right) y+x_1 \cdot x_2+y_1 \cdot y_2=0 \\ & x^2+y^2+2 a x+2 p y-b^2-q^2=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The parametric equations of the curve $$x^2+y^2+a x+b y=0$$ are
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & x^2+y^2+a x+b y=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(x+\frac{a}{2}\right)^2+\left(y+\frac{b}{2}\right)^2=\frac{a^2+b^2}{4} \end{aligned}$$

Comparing with $$(x-\mathrm{h})^2+(y-\mathrm{k})^2=\mathrm{r}^2$$, we get

$$\mathrm{h}=-\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}, \mathrm{k}=-\frac{\mathrm{b}}{2}, \mathrm{r}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2}{4}}$$

$$\therefore\quad$$ The parametric equations are

$$x=-\frac{a}{2}+\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{a}^2+b^2}{4}} \cos \theta, y=-\frac{b}{2}+\sqrt{\frac{a^2+b^2}{4}} \sin \theta$$

content: The circles $$x^2+y^2+2 \mathrm{a} x+\mathrm{c}=0$$ and $$x^2+y^2+2 b y+c=0$$ touch each other externally, if
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & x^2+y^2+2 a x+c=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2+2 a x+a^2+y^2=a^2-c \\ & \Rightarrow(x+a)^2+y^2=\left(\sqrt{a^2-c}\right)^2 \end{aligned}$$

i.e., it is a circle with centre $$(-a, 0)$$ and radius $$\sqrt{\mathrm{a}^2-\mathrm{c}}$$

Similarly,

$$\begin{aligned} & x^2+y^2+2 \mathrm{~b} y+\mathrm{c}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2+(y+\mathrm{b})^2=\left(\sqrt{\mathrm{b}^2-\mathrm{c}}\right)^2 \end{aligned}$$

i.e., it is a circle with centre $$(0,-b)$$ and radius $$=\sqrt{b^2-c}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ If circles touch externally, then we get

Sum of radii $$=$$ Distance between centres

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \sqrt{a^2-c}+\sqrt{b^2-c}=\sqrt{a^2+b^2} \\ & \Rightarrow a^2-c+b^2-c+2 \sqrt{a^2-c} \sqrt{b^2-c}=a^2+b^2 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(a^2-c\right)\left(b^2-c\right)=c^2 \\ & \Rightarrow a^2 b^2-c b^2-c a^2+c^2=c^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{a^2}+\frac{1}{b^2}=\frac{1}{c} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\lambda$$ is the perpendicular distance of a point $$\mathrm{P}$$ on the circle $$x^2+y^2+2 x+2 y-3=0$$, from the line $$2 x+y+13=0$$, then maximum possible value of $$\lambda$$ is
explanation:
Given equation of the circle is

$$x^2+y^2+2 x+2 y-3=0$$

Which can be written as: $$(x+1)^2+(y+1)^2=5$$

It is a circle with centre $$(-1,-1)$$ and radius $$\sqrt{5}$$

Given line is: $$2 x+y+13=0$$

To find the required distance, we find the equation of a line perpendicular to the given line, and passing through the centre of the given circle.

$$\therefore \quad$$ Equation of this line is: $$(y+1)=\frac{1}{2}(x+1)$$ i.e., $$x=2 y+1$$

Now, we find the points where line $$x=2 y+1$$ intersects the circle $$x^2+y^2+2 x+2 y-3=0$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & (2 y+1)^2+y^2+2(2 y+1)+2 y-3=0 \\ \therefore & 4 y^2+4 y+1+y^2+4 y+2+2 y-3=0 \\ \therefore & 5 y^2+10 y=0 \\ \therefore & y(y+2)=0 \\ \therefore & y=0 \text { or } y=-2 \\ \therefore & x=1 \text { or } x=-3 \end{array}$$

$$\therefore \quad(1,0)$$ and $$(-3,-2)$$ are the points on the circle, and one of them is at the maximum distance from the given line.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \mathrm{d}_1=\left|\frac{2(1)+(0)+13}{\sqrt{4+1}}\right| \text { and } \mathrm{d}_2=\left|\frac{2(-3)+(-2)+13}{\sqrt{4+1}}\right| \\ \therefore & \mathrm{d}_1=\frac{15}{\sqrt{5}}=3 \sqrt{5} \quad \text { and } \mathrm{d}_2=\frac{5}{\sqrt{5}}=\sqrt{5} \\ \therefore & \lambda=3 \sqrt{5} \end{array}$$

content: If a circle passes through points $$(4,0)$$ and $$(0,2)$$ and its centre lies on $$\mathrm{Y}$$-axis. If the radius of the circle is $$r$$, then the value of $$r^2-r+1$$ is
explanation:
Let $$(0, y)$$ be the centre of the circle.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \sqrt{(0-4)^2+(y-0)^2}=\sqrt{(0-0)^2+(y-2)^2} \\ \therefore & 16+y^2=(y-2)^2 \\ \therefore & 16+y^2=y^2-4 y+4 \\ \therefore & y=-3 \\ \therefore & \text { centre of the circle is }(0,-3) . \\ \therefore & \text { Radius of the circle }=\mathrm{r}=\sqrt{(0-0)^2+(-3-2)^2} \\ & =5 \text { units } \\ \therefore & \mathrm{r}^2-\mathrm{r}+1=25-5+1=21 \end{array}$$

content: If the circles $$x^2+y^2=9$$ and $$x^2+y^2+2 \alpha x+2 y+1=0$$ touch each other internally, then the value of $$\alpha^3$$ is

content: If the lines $$3 x-4 y-7=0$$ and $$2 x-3 y-5=0$$ pass through diameters of a circle of area $$49 \pi$$ square units, then the equation of the circle is
explanation:
The lines $$3x - 4y - 7 = 0$$ and $$2x - 3y - 5 = 0$$ are given to pass through the diameters of a circle. Since both lines are diameters of the circle, the point of intersection of the two lines will be the center of the circle.

To find the point of intersection, we can solve the two equations simultaneously. We can use the substitution or elimination method. Let's use the elimination method in this case by multiplying the first equation by 3 and the second by 4 so that the coefficients of $$y$$ match and can thus be eliminated.

Multiplying the first equation by 3:

$$3 \cdot (3x - 4y - 7) = 3 \cdot 0$$

$$9x - 12y - 21 = 0$$

Multiplying the second equation by 4:

$$4 \cdot (2x - 3y - 5) = 4 \cdot 0$$

$$8x - 12y - 20 = 0$$

Subtracting the second equation from the first:

$$9x - 12y - 21 - (8x - 12y - 20) = 0$$

$$x - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 1$$

Now we substitute $$x = 1$$ back into either original equation to solve for $$y$$. Using the first original equation:

$$3 \cdot 1 - 4y - 7 = 0$$

$$3 - 4y - 7 = 0$$

$$-4y = 4$$

$$y = -1$$

So the center of the circle is (1, -1).

The area of the circle is given by $$49\pi$$, which means that the radius squared, $$r^2$$, is:

$$\pi r^2 = 49\pi \Rightarrow r^2 = 49$$

The standard form of the equation of a circle with center (h, k) and radius r is given by:

$$ (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2 $$

Substituting the center (1, -1) and radius $$r = \sqrt{49} = 7$$ gives us:

$$ (x - 1)^2 + (y + 1)^2 = 7^2 $$

$$ x^2 - 2x + 1 + y^2 + 2y + 1 = 49 $$

Now we bring all terms to one side to set the equation equal to zero:

$$ x^2 + y^2 - 2x + 2y + 2 - 49 = 0 $$

$$ x^2 + y^2 - 2x + 2y - 47 = 0 $$

Therefore, the correct equation of the circle is:

Option A: $$ x^2 + y^2 - 2x + 2y - 47 = 0 $$

content: If $$y=2 x$$ is a chord of circle $$x^2+y^2-10 x=0$$, then the equation of circle with this chord as diameter is
explanation:

i.e. $$x(x-2)=0 \Rightarrow x=0,2 \Rightarrow y=0,4$$

Thus end points of the chord are $$(0,0)$$ and $$(2,4)$$

Mid point of the chord $$=\left(\frac{2}{2}, \frac{4}{2}\right)=(1,2)$$ and length of chord $$=\sqrt{(2)^2+(4)^2}=\sqrt{20}$$ is the diameter of required circle.

i.e. $$x^2+y^2-2 x-4 y=0$$

content: The equation of common tangent to the circles $$x^2+y^2-4 x+10 y+20=0$$ and $$x^2+y^2+8 x-6 y-24=0$$ is
explanation:
Circle $$x^2+y^2-4 x+10 y+20=0$$ has centre $$C_1=(2,-5)$$ and radius $$r_1=\sqrt{4+25-20}=3$$

Circle $$x^2+y^2+8 x-6 y-24=0$$ has centre $$C_2=(-4,3)$$ and radius $$r_2=\sqrt{16+9+24}=7$$

Distance between centres

content: If a circle passes through the points $$(0,0),(x, 0)$$ and $$(0, y)$$, then the coordinates of its centre are
explanation:
To find the coordinates of the center of the circle passing through the points $$(0,0), (x, 0)$$, and $$(0, y)$$, we can follow these steps.

First, let's understand that the general equation of a circle with center $$(h, k)$$ and radius $$r$$ can be written as:

$$ (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2 $$

Since the circle passes through the origin $$(0,0)$$, substituting $$(0,0)$$ in the above equation, we get:

$$ h^2 + k^2 = r^2 \quad \text{(1)} $$

Next, since the circle passes through $$(x,0)$$, substituting $$(x,0)$$ in the circle equation, we get:

$$ (x - h)^2 + k^2 = r^2 \quad \text{(2)} $$

Substituting $$r^2$$ from equation (1) into equation (2) gives:

$$ (x - h)^2 + k^2 = h^2 + k^2 $$

Canceling $$k^2$$ from both sides, we get:

$$ (x - h)^2 = h^2 $$

Solving for $$h$$, we get two possible solutions:

$$ x - h = h \quad \text{or} \quad x - h = -h $$

$$ x = 2h \quad \text{or} \quad x = 0 $$

Since $$x \neq 0$$ in our case, we have:

$$ h = \frac{x}{2} $$

Similarly, since the circle passes through $$(0, y)$$, substituting $$(0, y)$$ into the circle equation, we get:

$$ h^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2 \quad \text{(3)} $$

Substituting $$r^2$$ from equation (1) into equation (3) gives:

$$ h^2 + (y - k)^2 = h^2 + k^2 $$

Canceling $$h^2$$ from both sides, we get:

$$ (y - k)^2 = k^2 $$

Solving for $$k$$, we get two possible solutions:

$$ y - k = k \quad \text{or} \quad y - k = -k $$

$$ y = 2k \quad \text{or} \quad y = 0 $$

Since $$y \neq 0$$ in our case, we have:

$$ k = \frac{y}{2} $$

Therefore, the coordinates of the center of the circle are:

$$ \left(\frac{x}{2}, \frac{y}{2}\right) $$

So, the correct answer is:

Option B

$$ \left(\frac{x}{2}, \frac{y}{2}\right) $$

content: Two circles centred at $$(2,3)$$ and $$(4,5)$$ intersects each other. If their radii are equal, then the equation of the common chord is

content: The equation of a circle that passes through the origin and cut off intercepts $$-2$$ and 3 on the $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis and $$\mathrm{Y}$$-axis respectively is
explanation:
To determine the correct equation of the circle that passes through the origin and intercepts the x-axis at -2 and the y-axis at 3, let's start by understanding the general form of the equation of a circle in a Cartesian coordinate system. The general form is:

$$x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0$$

Here, the center of the circle is $(-g, -f)$ and the radius is given by $\sqrt{g^2 + f^2 - c}$.

Given that the circle passes through the origin (0,0), we can substitute these coordinates into the general equation to find $c$. Substituting $(0,0)$, we get:

$$0^2 + 0^2 + 2g \cdot 0 + 2f \cdot 0 + c = 0$$

This simplifies to:

$$c = 0$$

So the equation simplifies to:

$$x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy = 0$$

Next, let's use the intercepts on the x-axis and y-axis to determine $g$ and $f$. The intercept on the x-axis is -2, so the circle passes through the point (-2, 0). Substituting these coordinates into the equation:

$$(-2)^2 + 0^2 + 2g(-2) + 2f(0) = 0$$

Which simplifies to:

$$4 - 4g = 0 \implies g = 1$$

Similarly, the intercept on the y-axis is 3, so the circle passes through the point (0, 3). Substituting these coordinates into the equation:

$$0^2 + 3^2 + 2g(0) + 2f(3) = 0$$

Which simplifies to:

$$9 + 6f = 0 \implies f = -\frac{3}{2}$$

Thus, substituting $g = 1$ and $f = -\frac{3}{2}$ into the general equation of the circle, we get:

$$x^2 + y^2 + 2(1)x + 2\left(-\frac{3}{2}\right)y = 0$$

This further simplifies to:

$$x^2 + y^2 + 2x - 3y = 0$$

Therefore, the correct equation of the circle is option C:

$$x^2 + y^2 + 2x - 3y = 0$$

content: The equation of circle with centre at $$(2,-3)$$ and the circumference $$10 \pi$$ units is
explanation:

content: The equation of the circle whose centre lies on the line $$x-4 y=1$$ and which passes through the points $$(3,7)$$ and $$(5,5)$$ is
explanation:
Let $$(h, k) b$$ the centre of the circle. It lies on the line $$x-4 y=1 \Rightarrow h=1+4 k$$

$$\therefore$$ centre $$\equiv(4 \mathrm{k}+1, \mathrm{k})$$

Circle passes through points $$(3,7)$$ and $$(5,5)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore(4 \mathrm{k}+1-5)^2+(\mathrm{k}-5)^2 \quad=(4 \mathrm{k}+1-3)^2+(\mathrm{k}-7)^2 \\ & \therefore(4 \mathrm{k}-4)^2+(\mathrm{k}-5)^2=(4 \mathrm{k}-2)^2+(\mathrm{k}-7)^2 \\ & \therefore 16 \mathrm{k}^2+16-32 \mathrm{k}+\mathrm{k}^2+25-10 \mathrm{k}=16 \mathrm{k}^2+4-16 \mathrm{k}+\mathrm{k}^2+49-14 \mathrm{k} \\ & \therefore-42 \mathrm{k}+41=-30 \mathrm{k}+53 \Rightarrow 12 \mathrm{k}=-12 \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=-1 \\ & \therefore \text { Centre } \equiv(-3,-1) \\ & \therefore \text { Radius } \quad=\sqrt{(-3-5)^2+(-1-5)^2}=10 \end{aligned}$$

Hence equation of required circle is

$$(x+3)^2+(y+1)^2=(10)^2 \text { i.e. } x^2+y^2+6 x+2 y-90=0$$

content: If the lines $$3x - 4y + 4 = 0$$ and $$6x - 8y - 7 = 0$$ are tangents to a circle, then the radius of the circle is
explanation:
We have, $$3 x-4 y+4=0$$ ....(1) and $$6 x-8 y-7=0 \Rightarrow 3 x-4 y-\frac{7}{2}=0$$ ..... (2)

Lines (1) and (2) are parallel to one another and these lines are tangents to the circle.

$$\therefore$$ Distance between the lines is equal to diameter of circle.

$$\therefore$$ $$D = {{\left| {4 - \left( {{{ - 7} \over 2}} \right)} \right|} \over {\sqrt {{{(3)}^2} + {{( - 4)}^2}} }} = {{\left( {4 + {7 \over 2}} \right)} \over {\sqrt {25} }} = {{15} \over {2(5)}} = {3 \over 2} \Rightarrow $$ radius $$=\frac{3}{4}$$ units

content: The radius of the circle passing through the points $$(5,7),(2,-2)$$ and $$(-2,0)$$ is
explanation:
Given, point $$(5,7),(2,-2)$$ and $$(-2,0)$$ lie on circle

Let $$(h, k)$$ be centre of circle

$$\therefore \quad O A=O C$$

$$\therefore \quad(h+2)^2+k^2=(h-5)^2+(k-7)^2$$

$$\begin{array}{ccc} \Rightarrow & h^2+4 h+4+k^2 \\ & =h^2-10 h+25+k^2-14 k+49 \\ \Rightarrow & 14 h+14 k=70 \\ & h+k=5 \quad \text{..... (i)} \end{array}$$

When, $$O A=O B$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore(h+2)^2+k^2=(h-2)^2+(k+2)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow h^2+4 h+4+k^2=h^2-4 h+4+k^2+4 k+4 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 8 h-4 k=4 \\ & 2 h-k=1 \quad \text{..... (ii)}\\ \end{aligned}$$

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$h=2, k=3$$

$$\therefore$$ Centre of circle $$(2,3)$$

Radius of circle $$O A=\sqrt{(2+2)^2+3^2}$$

$$O A=\sqrt{4^2+3^2}=\sqrt{16+9}=\sqrt{25}=5$$units

content: The intercept on the line $y=x$ by the circle $x^2+y^2-2 x=0$ is $A B$. The equation of the circle with $A B$ as a diameter is .............
explanation:
We have equation of line $y=x$ and equation of circle $x^2+y^2-2 x=0$

Now, intersecting points of given line and circle,

$$\begin{aligned} & & x^2+x^2-2 x & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & & 2 x^2-2 x & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & & 2 x(x-1) & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & & x & =0,1 \end{aligned}$$

when $x=0$ then $y=0$ and when $x=1$ then $y=1$

$\therefore$ Coordinates of end points of diameter $A B$ are $(0,0)$ and $(1,1)$.

$\therefore$ Required equation of circle with diameter $A B$

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} & & (x-0)(x-1)+(y-0)(y-1) & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & & x^2-2+y^2-y=0 \\ \Rightarrow & & x^2+y^2-x-y=0 \end{array}$$

content: The equation of the circle concentric with the circle $x^2+y^2-6 x-4 y-12=0$ and touching the $Y$-axis is ............
explanation:
Given equation of circle

$$x^2+y^2-6 x-4 y-12=0\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

Centre of circle $(i)$ is $(3,2)$

Equation of circle concentric with circle (i) and touching the $Y$-axis is

$$\begin{aligned} \quad(x-3)^2+(y-2)^2 & =(3)^2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad x^2+9-6 x+y^2+4-4 y & =9 \\ \Rightarrow \quad x^2+y^2-6 x-4 y+4 & =0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of the differential equation of all circles having centre at $A(-1,2)$ is ........
explanation:
General equation of a circle with centre $(-g,-f)$ is

$$\begin{gathered} x^2+y^2+2 g x+2 f y+c=0 \\ \text{If}\quad(-g,-f)=A(-1,2) \end{gathered}$$

Then equation of circle,

$$x^2+y^2+2 x-4 y+c=0$$
